records.ety: [F. contrefait, p. p. of contrefaire to counterfeit; contre (L. contra) + faire to make, fr. L. facere. See Counter, adv., and Fact.]

defs: Representing by imitation or likeness; having a resemblance to something else; portrayed.
quotes: Look here upon this picture, and on this- The counterfeit presentment of two brothers.

defs: Fabricated in imitation of something else, with a view to defraud by passing the false copy for genuine or original; as, counterfeit antiques; counterfeit coin.
Assuming the appearance of something; false; spurious; deceitful; hypocritical; as, a counterfeit philanthropist.
That which resembles or is like another thing; a likeness; a portrait; a counterpart.
quotes: Thou drawest a counterfeit Best in all Athens.
Even Nature's self envied the same, And grudged to see the counterfeit should shame The thing itself.

defs: That which is made in imitation of something, with a view to deceive by passing the false for the true; as, the bank note was a counterfeit.
quotes: Never call a true piece of gold a counterfeit.
Some of these counterfeits are fabricated with such exquisite taste and skill, that it is the achievement of criticism to distinguish them from originals.

defs: One who pretends to be what he is not; one who personates another; an impostor; a cheat.
quotes: I fear thou art another counterfeit; And yet, in faith, thou bear'st thee like a king.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Counterfeited; p. pr. & vb. n. Counterfeiting.]

defs: To imitate, or put on a semblance of; to mimic; as, to counterfeit the voice of another person.
quotes: Full well they laughed with counterfeited glee At all his jokes, for many a joke had he.

defs: To imitate with a view to deceiving, by passing the copy for that which is original or genuine; to forge; as, to counterfeit the signature of another, coins, notes, etc.
To carry on a deception; to dissemble; to feign; to pretend.
quotes: The knave counterfeits well; a good knave.
